question: The term critical value is often used to refer to the value of a test statistic that determines statistical significance at some fixed $\alpha$ level for a test. For example, $\pm 1.96$ are the critical values for a two-tailed $z$ test at $\alpha=0.05$. In performing a $t$ test based on 21 observations, what are the critical values for a one-tailed test when $\alpha=0.05$ ? That is, what values of the $t_{	ext {stat }}$ will give a onesided $P$-value that is less than or equal to 0.05 ? What are the critical values for a two-tailed test at $\alpha=0.05$ ?
answer: First note that $d f=21-1=20$. For a one-sided test we get a $P$-value less than 0.05 when the $t_{\text {stat }}$ is either less than -1.725 or more than 1.725 , that is, $\left|t_{\text {stat }}\right| \geq 1.725$. For a two-tailed test, we get a $P$-value less than 0.05 when the $t_{\text {stat }}$ is either less than -2.086 or more than 2.086, that is, $\left|t_{\text {stat }}\right| \geq 2.086$.

meta info.caption: Critical values for a t statistic.
